title: Reconciling competing stock assessments for jumbo flying squid through probabilistic risk analysis
text:
NoteCite this work



Quiroz, J.-C., & Gozzer, R. (2026). Reconciling competing stock assessments for jumbo flying squid through probabilistic risk analysis. Zenodo. https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.21361536
The DOI above is the concept DOI: it always resolves to the most recent version. To pin this exact deposit instead, cite 10.5281/zenodo.21361537.

title: Reconciling competing stock assessments for jumbo flying squid through probabilistic risk analysis
section: CALAMASUR — two-stage environment-driven depletion + production model
text:
CALAMASUR — two-stage environment-driven depletion + production model
The CALAMASUR assessment (Roa-Ureta & Wiff, 2025), presented as observer paper SC13-Obs03, is the most mechanistic of the three. It is a two-stage, environment-driven approach spanning 1990–2024. Stage 1 fits multi-annual, multi-fleet generalized depletion models to a monthly database (January 2012–December 2024) of total catch, fishing effort, and mean weight for three fleets (Peruvian, Chilean, Asian), covering >99% of regional landings. Stage 2 then drives a Pella-Tomlinson surplus-production model with El Niño/La Niña transitions (NOAA’s Oceanic Niño Index; 10 transitions over 1990–2024), testing 16 hypotheses about how carrying capacity, production symmetry, and intrinsic growth rate change with the environment.
Crucially, the depletion stage is effort-driven rather than CPUE-driven, so the assessment does not rely on a standardized CPUE series as an index of abundance — an assumption widely questioned for fast-growing, highly migratory squid. Natural mortality is estimated inside the model at ≈0.17/month (≈2.0/yr), consistent with the species’ short life cycle. The best-supported environmental hypothesis is that only the intrinsic growth rate r varies with the environment — higher in El Niño (2.93/yr) than in La Niña (0.48/yr). The compiled ADMB production model (a two-regime Pella-Tomlinson fit) estimates a carrying capacity K ≈ 10.85 million tonnes and a shape parameter p ≈ 1.97. 
The estimated biomass fluctuates widely (maxima an order of magnitude above minima) but shows no sustained downward trend, with the highest peaks before 2019; exploitation rates have risen since 2020 but remain mostly below 40% (Figure 2). The main conclusion is that the stock is not overfished, though it may be undergoing overfishing. Because the stock has entered a regime of strong environment-driven fluctuations, MSY and BMSY are judged inadequate reference points, a standard Kobe plot cannot be built, and estimates of sustainable harvest rates (mean latent productivity) remain statistically imprecise.






Figure 2: CALAMASUR estimated trajectories of biomass, mortality, and exploitation rate. Biomass fluctuates widely without a sustained trend, while the exploitation rate has risen since 2020 but remains mostly below 40%.

title: Reconciling competing stock assessments for jumbo flying squid through probabilistic risk analysis
text:
Chile (IFOP) — SPiCT surplus-production model
The Chilean assessment (Payá, 2025), presented as SC13-SQ06, applies the Stochastic Production model in Continuous Time (SPiCT) — a state-space Pella-Tomlinson surplus-production model that separates process from observation error and can be run at monthly/quarterly steps — to D. gigas in FAO Area 87. Its main advance over previous assessments is the use of data up to 2024, reducing the two-year lag of earlier work. Six country CPUE indices were compiled (two Chinese fleet indices, Chinese Taipei, Korea, Chile, Peru), and a sensitivity analysis of seven cases tested a single global index versus six country indices, a fixed Schaefer curve, and a prior on r, over two data spans (2001–2024 and 2012–2024).
Catch fell by ~52% in 2024, and the abundance indices show a general decreasing trend. MSY converges around ~950 kt (906–950 kt across cases). The 2025 status is uncertain — of seven cases, four fell in the Kobe red zone, one yellow, two green — but the cases fitted to the six country indices, and in particular Case 7 (free productivity curve, six indices, r prior) with the best retrospective pattern, place the 2025 stock in the Kobe red zone, with a 2026 TAC of ~467 kt (comparable to the 2024 catch). TAC advice under FMSY ranged widely, from 267 kt to 989 kt across cases. This is a notable contrast with the “healthy” 2022 status discussed in 2024, and the reversal is driven by a 52% catch drop and declining indices. The authors caution that SPiCT assumes indices are unbiased indicators of the whole stock, and recommend standardising CPUE and accounting for the three D. gigas phenotypes and environmentally driven variability.

title: Reconciling competing stock assessments for jumbo flying squid through probabilistic risk analysis
section: China (Shanghai Ocean University) — Bayesian state-space model with ENSO
text:
China (Shanghai Ocean University) — Bayesian state-space model with ENSO
The Chinese assessment (Li & Chen, 2025), presented as SC13-SQ07, is a Bayesian state-space surplus-production (Pella-Tomlinson) model fitted in WinBUGS via MCMC, with an explicit test of how the ENSO cycle affects population dynamics. Four abundance indices were used: Peruvian and Chilean CPUE from CALAMASUR, plus the Chinese squid-jigging CPUE standardized with a Generalized Additive Model (GAM) using environmental covariates (SST, chlorophyll-a, salinity, Niño 1+2) and spatiotemporal terms. Seven environment-dependent models were tested, letting carrying capacity K, growth rate r, and shape s vary between El Niño and La Niña phases.
The GAM confirmed year, month, and salinity as the main CPUE drivers, with standardized CPUE peaking in 2015 and lowest in 2024. Among the seven models, the “Kr” model (both K and r vary with ENSO) had the lowest DIC and was best supported. Base-scenario MSY ≈ 1.19–1.35 Mt (traditional model), rising to ~1.3–1.6 Mt under the environment-dependent model. Kobe diagnostics indicate the stock has been sustainable since 2013 — neither overfished nor undergoing overfishing — with a terminal-year probability of healthy status of 70.9–88.3% under the environment-dependent model (58.6–73.1% under the traditional one). Notably, despite the sharp 2024 landings decline, the stock stayed healthy, a pattern the authors attribute to El Niño’s influence on population dynamics rather than to depletion.

title: Update of the SPiCT applied to Dosidicus gigas in FAO Area 87
text: A stock assessment paper by Chile (Payá 2025), presented as SC13-SQ06 to the 13th Meeting of the SPRFMO Scientific Committee (8–13 September 2025, Wellington, New Zealand). Dated September 2025.

title: Update of the SPiCT applied to Dosidicus gigas in FAO Area 87
text: A stock assessment paper by Chile (Payá 2025), presented as SC13-SQ06 to the 13th Meeting of the SPRFMO Scientific Committee (8–13 September 2025, Wellington, New Zealand). Dated September 2025.

title: Update of the SPiCT applied to Dosidicus gigas in FAO Area 87
text:
2 Subject
An updated stock assessment of jumbo squid (Dosidicus gigas) in FAO Area 87 — which spans the SPRFMO high-seas area plus the Ecuadorian, Peruvian and Chilean EEZs. Its main advance over previous assessments is the use of data up to 2024, reducing the two-year delay of earlier work and bringing the assessment closer to in-season conditions.

title: Update of the SPiCT applied to Dosidicus gigas in FAO Area 87
text:
3 Methods
The assessment applies the Stochastic Production model in Continuous Time (SPiCT) — a state-space, Pella-Tomlinson surplus production model that separates random stock-dynamics variability from observation error and allows assessment at monthly/quarterly steps. Six country CPUE abundance indices were compiled (two Chinese fleet indices, Chinese Taipei, Korea, Chile, Peru), with an updated Chinese index provided by Chinese scientists. A sensitivity analysis of seven cases tested the effect of using a single global index versus six country-specific indices, fixing the Schaefer production curve, and applying a prior on the intrinsic growth rate r. Two data spans were compared: full series (2001–2024) and short series (2012–2024).

title: Update of the SPiCT applied to Dosidicus gigas in FAO Area 87
text:
4 Key results

Total squid catch fell sharply — a 52% reduction in 2024 — and the abundance indices show a general decreasing trend.
Biomass rose from 2001 to ~2010, then declined; fishing mortality (F) shows the inverse pattern, rising steeply in recent years.
r and K varied widely across cases and were negatively correlated, yielding an MSY of ~950 kt (range 906–950 kt across cases). r estimates (~0.8–2.4) sit in the range expected for a fast-growing, short-lived squid when priors are used.
2025 stock status is uncertain: of the seven cases, four fell in the Kobe red zone, one yellow, two green. Cases fitted to the six country indices placed the stock in the red zone.
Case 7 (free productivity curve, six indices, r prior) had the best retrospective pattern and estimated a 2025 status in the red zone with a 2026 TAC of 467 kt (similar to the 2024 catch).
2026 TAC under FMSY ranged from 267 kt (Case 6) to 989 kt (Case 1).
The short-series (2012–2024) sensitivity gave similar MSY (941–950 kt) and TAC (271–756 kt); two of seven cases did not converge, and most placed 2025 in the red zone.

title: Update of the SPiCT applied to Dosidicus gigas in FAO Area 87
text:
5 Main conclusion
Stock status is highly uncertain but the most reliable case (best retrospective pattern) places the 2025 stock in the Kobe red zone — i.e. potentially overfished and/or undergoing overfishing — a notable contrast with the “healthy” 2022 status discussed in 2024, driven by the 52% catch drop and declining indices. MSY converges around ~950 kt, but reference points and TAC advice remain sensitive to the choice of abundance index.

title: Update of the SPiCT applied to Dosidicus gigas in FAO Area 87
text:
6 Way forward
Improve and standardize the CPUE abundance indices (incorporating spatiotemporal variability and different squid sizes across regions), since SPiCT assumes indices are unbiased indicators of the whole stock. Account for the three phenotypes of D. gigas (which likely have distinct growth rates and carrying capacities) and for environmentally driven interannual variability in productivity, which the single-stock process-error assumption may not fully capture.

title: Update of the SPiCT applied to Dosidicus gigas in FAO Area 87
text:
References


Payá, Ignacio S. 2025. Update of the Stochastic Production Model in Continuous Time (SPiCT) Applied to Dosidicus gigas in the FAO Area 87. Scientific Committee Document Nos. SC13-SQ06. Instituto de Fomento Pesquero (IFOP), Stock Assessment Department.

title: About
text:
Given the complexity of the decision process and the technical disagreements between the squid models, three non-exclusive lines of action are proposed. Each aims to give Scientific Committee members information that complements — rather than replaces — the population models currently in play.

 Line 1 · Under way Multi-model risk analysis Weights population projections from a set of models (CALAMASUR, Chile, China) under differing assumptions about fishing effort. Its defining feature is the capacity to incorporate the views of a broad group of stakeholders, and to weight the credibility of each projection on data, empirical evidence and expert judgement. 
 Line 2 · Proposed Empirical Management Strategy Evaluation Applies the technical and methodological basis of a standard MSE to test, by simulation, how different models respond to alternative harvest control rules. Models that lack robustness show little sensitivity across control rules — and can therefore be set aside. No results yet — this line is proposed, not started. 
 Line 3 · Committed Improving the CALAMASUR model Delivers the commitments made in SC13-Obs03: widening the fishing units (fleets) represented, and capturing differences in natural mortality between zones. 



The three lines answer different questions, and are deliberately not alternatives to one another:









Question it answers
Relationship to the models




Multi-model risk analysis
Given that the models disagree, what can we still say with confidence?
Carries all three models as weighted hypotheses


Empirical MSE
Which models are robust enough to inform a decision?
Tests the models against harvest control rules


Model improvement
Can the remaining objections to CALAMASUR’s model be closed out?
Improves one model



Line 1 is the most developed: it is set out in full in the working paper, which defines risk following FAO guidance as “the probability of something undesirable happening”, and shows how competing hypotheses are hierarchized, weighted, and combined into probability statements that can seed a harvest control rule.

title: About
text:
Implementation
The immediate objective is to develop at least one of these lines far enough to present an implementation scheme — structured along the lines of terms of reference — to the 14th Meeting of the SPRFMO Scientific Committee (SC14, September 2026).
The lines differ in complexity and in the level of technical dedication they demand. For SC14 the realistic aim is therefore to set out the basis for implementation schematically, rather than to deliver all three in full.
The work is organised around three steps:

 Step 1 · Before the meeting Technical document Preparation of the technical document, together with the supporting material used to present it — agreed with the counterpart before submission. 
 Step 2 · During Representation at SC14 Scientific representation of CALAMASUR through the meeting of the SPRFMO Scientific Committee, September 2026. 
 Step 3 · After Post-meeting review A retrospective assessment of how the position was received, used to identify and scope the next round of technical actions. 

A detailed explanation of the methodological schemes behind each line of action can be provided and discussed on request.

title: About
text:
Who
This work is carried out by Drs Juan-Carlos Quiroz (ORCID) with Renato Gozzer (ORCID), and is funded by CALAMASUR with support from Facile Visual and the Innovations for Ocean Action Foundation.
The working paper is archived and citable at 10.5281/zenodo.21361536.

title: Jumbo Flying Squid Models
text:
Three stock-assessment models were presented for the Southeast Pacific jumbo flying squid (Dosidicus gigas). Each takes a different modelling approach and data source; select a card to open its summary.

 CALAMASUR · Peru Calamasur stock assessment Stock assessment of jumbo flying squid with an environment-driven biomass dynamics model. 
 SC13-SQ06 · Chile SPiCT — FAO area 87 Update of the SPiCT surplus-production model applied to Dosidicus gigas in FAO area 87. 
 SC13-SQ07 · China State-space model & ENSO State-space biomass dynamics assessment examining the impacts of ENSO on the stock.

title: Jumbo Flying Squid Models
text:
Three stock-assessment models were presented for the Southeast Pacific jumbo flying squid (Dosidicus gigas). Each takes a different modelling approach and data source; select a card to open its summary.

 CALAMASUR · Peru Calamasur stock assessment Stock assessment of jumbo flying squid with an environment-driven biomass dynamics model. 
 SC13-SQ06 · Chile SPiCT — FAO area 87 Update of the SPiCT surplus-production model applied to Dosidicus gigas in FAO area 87. 
 SC13-SQ07 · China State-space model & ENSO State-space biomass dynamics assessment examining the impacts of ENSO on the stock.

title: Jumbo Flying Squid Models
section: How the Chile, China & CALAMASUR Models Differ
text:
How the Chile, China & CALAMASUR Models Differ

Why the three assessments disagree
All three groups assessed the same species (Dosidicus gigas), the same region (Southeast Pacific / FAO Area 87), and broadly the same catch data updated to 2024 — including the sharp ~52% drop in landings in 2024. Yet they reached different verdicts on stock status. The divergence comes almost entirely from model architecture and how each model treats the 2024 catch collapse and environmental variability, not from the underlying data. The sections below walk through where the models agree and where they part ways.


The three modelling approaches at a glance









Dimension
Chile — SPiCT (Payá 2025)
China — Bayesian state-space (Li and Chen 2025)
CALAMASUR — Depletion + Pella-Tomlinson (Roa-Ureta and Wiff 2025)




Model family
Surplus production (Pella-Tomlinson) in continuous time, state-space
Surplus production (Pella-Tomlinson) in discrete (annual) time, state-space
Two-stage: monthly generalized depletion model → Pella-Tomlinson surplus production


Estimation
Maximum likelihood (TMB)
Bayesian MCMC (WinBUGS)
Non-Bayesian hierarchical (ADMB)


Time step
Continuous (month/quarter capable)
Annual
Stage 1 monthly; Stage 2 annual


Data used
Catch + 6 country CPUE indices (or 1 global)
Catch + 4 fleet indices (Peru, Chile, Asia)
Monthly catch, effort & mean weight (3 fleets) + FAO annual landings


Uses fishing effort?
No (index-based)
No (index-based)
Yes — depletion model is effort-driven, not CPUE-driven


Environment in model
Not explicit (discussed as caveat)
Explicit — K, r, s vary by ENSO phase; 7 models tested
Explicit — r (and tested K, s) vary by El Niño/La Niña; 16 hypotheses


Time span
2001–2024 (and 2012–2024)
2012–2024
2012–2024 (Stage 1); 1990–2024 (Stage 2)


2025/terminal verdict
Kobe red zone (best case)
Healthy since 2013 (70–88% prob.)
Not overfished, possibly overfishing





Where they fundamentally differ

1. Depletion model vs. pure production model
The single biggest structural difference is CALAMASUR’s two-stage depletion approach. Chile (SPiCT) and China both fit a surplus production model directly to catch and a CPUE abundance index — they infer biomass from the relationship between catch and a relative abundance signal. CALAMASUR instead first fits a generalized depletion model at monthly steps that uses fishing effort (not CPUE) as the mechanistic driver, tracking within-season depletion and recruitment pulses for each fleet; the biomass series it produces then feeds a Pella-Tomlinson model at Stage 2.
This matters because production models assume the CPUE index is proportional (or a known function) of true abundance — an assumption widely questioned for fast-growing, highly migratory squid. CALAMASUR’s design deliberately avoids relying on standardized CPUE as an abundance index, estimating natural mortality inside the model instead (≈2.0/yr, consistent with the short life cycle).


2. How the environment enters
China and CALAMASUR both build the ENSO cycle into the model, letting productivity parameters switch between El Niño and La Niña regimes — China favoured a model where both carrying capacity K and growth rate r vary (the “Kr” model), while CALAMASUR found best support for only r varying (higher in El Niño: 2.93/yr vs 0.48/yr in La Niña). Chile’s SPiCT run does not encode the environment explicitly; the authors instead flag environmental variability and the three squid phenotypes as caveats to be addressed in future work.


3. What each does with the 2024 catch drop
This is the crux of the differing verdicts:

China interprets the healthy stock despite the 2024 landings collapse as evidence of an El Niño effect on population dynamics — the drop reflects environment/availability, not depletion. Verdict: still healthy.
CALAMASUR sees a stock in a regime of wide fluctuations with no sustained downward biomass trend and exploitation mostly below 40%; not overfished, but the rising exploitation rate means overfishing may be occurring, and MSY/BMSY are judged inadequate reference points.
Chile reads the 52% catch reduction plus a general decline in the abundance indices as a genuine downturn, placing the 2025 stock in the Kobe red zone (in the best-fitting, six-index case). SPiCT trusts the declining CPUE signal more directly.



4. Reference points and management outputs
Chile’s SPiCT produces conventional Kobe-plot status (B/BMSY, F/FMSY) and explicit 2026 TAC advice (267–989 kt across cases; ~467 kt in the best case). China produces Kobe status with terminal-year healthy-status probabilities (58–88%). CALAMASUR argues that under strong fluctuations MSY and BMSY are not valid reference points and a standard Kobe plot cannot be built, calling instead for new environment-aware reference points based on mean latent productivity (still imprecise).



Points of genuine agreement
Despite the different headlines, the three assessments converge on several things: the stock is heavily influenced by the ENSO cycle; biomass fluctuates widely rather than showing a clean trend; exploitation has been rising in recent years; and the standardized CPUE indices need improvement (spatiotemporal structure, multiple phenotypes/sizes). Both China and CALAMASUR independently conclude that environmental cycles drive productivity, and even Chile — while not modelling it — names the three phenotypes and environmental variability as the key unresolved issues.


How to read the disagreement
The spread of verdicts is best understood as a model-structure spectrum rather than a data dispute:

The more a model trusts declining CPUE as a direct abundance signal (Chile, SPiCT), the more pessimistic the status.
The more a model attributes the 2024 drop to environment/availability (China), the more optimistic the status.
The more a model relies on effort-based depletion dynamics and treats MSY as unreliable under fluctuations (CALAMASUR), the more it lands on a cautious middle — “not overfished, watch the exploitation rate.”

No single model is definitively “right”; each encodes a different, defensible hypothesis about why catches fell in 2024. That is precisely why the SPRFMO Squid Working Group runs them in parallel.

title: Jumbo Flying Squid Models
text:
Sources

Update of the SPiCT applied to Dosidicus gigas in FAO area 87
Assessment of jumbo squid in Southeast Pacific Ocean based on state-space biomass dynamics model and impacts of ENSO
Stock assessment of jumbo flying squid

title: Jumbo Flying Squid
text:
Jumbo Squid

Why three stock assessments of the same squid disagree — and how probabilistic risk analysis can reconcile them.
Explore Learn more

title: Jumbo Flying Squid
text:
Context
Over the past three years, CALAMASUR has built a consolidated scientific position in favour of precautionary management of the jumbo flying squid fishery under the South Pacific Regional Fisheries Management Organisation (SPRFMO).
Recent discussions within the SPRFMO Scientific Committee aimed at reducing fishing effort on jumbo squid have produced measures that do not explicitly guarantee a reduction in fishing pressure on the species. The work described on this site exists to reopen that discussion on evidence-based terms, and to continue CALAMASUR’s scientific contribution to the elements that underpin management decisions for this fishery.

title: Jumbo Flying Squid
text:
Where the science stands
CALAMASUR presented an improved assessment of jumbo squid to the SPRFMO Scientific Committee in 2025 (SC13-Obs03), covering a range of exploitation scenarios in response to technical observations raised by Committee members.

The results showed that, although the population trend is not necessarily declining, jumbo squid is subject to a high exploitation rate.

Because these results do not coincide with the assessments presented by Chile and China, it has proved difficult to agree a consensus exploitation status supported by model results.
Refining the CALAMASUR model further — distributing effort across fleets, discriminating differences in population parameters such as natural mortality — remains necessary. But even a model that answers most of the Committee’s questions would not, on its own, resolve the deeper need: identifying and adopting a base model against which exploitation status is determined.
CALAMASUR plans to address this problem through three long-term lines of action (see About+), each designed to provide Scientific Committee members with information that complements — rather than replaces — the population models currently in use. This website tracks progress on the first of these lines, Multi-model risk analysis, whose initial outcomes are presented in the working paper and the SCW18 presentation.

title: Jumbo Flying Squid
text:
Summary

A risk-assessment framework is presented to identify sustainable management actions for the jumbo flying squid (Dosidicus gigas) in the South-Eastern Pacific Ocean. It applies reference-point-based management quantities inside a probabilistic setup that accommodates multiple, competing hypotheses drawn from the current stock-assessment models developed under the umbrella of the South Pacific Regional Fisheries Management Organisation (SPRFMO). By explicitly quantifying uncertainty instead of relying on point estimates from a single “best” assessment model, the framework reconciles the tension between competing candidate models used for inference and decision-making. The main definition under this framework is grounded in the FAO Guidelines (FAO 1995) where risk is defined as “the probability of something undesirable happening” — in this case, exceeding the reference points that trigger management action.
Three SPRFMO parties have presented stock assessment methodologies for jumbo flying squid for discussion by the 2025 SPRFMO Scientific Committee. The CALAMASUR approach is the most mechanistic, operating at monthly time steps and explicitly modeling fleet-specific depletion and fishing operations before scaling up to a production model (Roa-Ureta and Wiff 2025). The Chilean SPiCT model is more data-modest, relying on annual CPUE indices without requiring fishing effort at fine temporal resolution, and uses frequentist maximum likelihood (Payá 2025). The Chinese model is fully Bayesian, with the additional step of formally standardising CPUE via GAM before incorporating it into the state-space model, and uniquely focuses on MCMC-based posterior inference (Li and Chen 2025).
The proposed risk-assessment framework is a compromise among model complexity and statistical rigour, weighting models according to the information contained in the data, population and fishery process, and expert judgement, while acknowledging that the complexity of jumbo flying squid stock assessments can preclude strict adherence to ideal statistical criteria for management decision-making. In fact, the CALAMASUR model indicates the stock is not overfished but may be experiencing overfishing; the Chilean SPiCT model offers a more pessimistic and uncertain assessment; by contrast, the Chinese Bayesian state-space model is the most optimistic, finding the stock has remained sustainable since 2013 with no evidence of overfishing or being overfished. The three assessments arrive at markedly different conclusions despite using overlapping data from the same fishery; these differences reflect their methodological approaches and make it difficult to take discrete management decisions such as setting a TAC, opening or closing a season, or allocating effort across fleets.
To address these challenges, the risk‑assessment framework yields probabilistic statements about whether feasible reference points — such as targets and limits used in harvest control rules — have been, or are likely to be, exceeded under current or alternative management actions. Those probabilities are derived through a hierarchical evaluation of competing hypotheses, including alternative stock‑assessment models, multiple metrics of model reliability and performance, and the exclusion of implausible scenarios. What makes this framework particularly viable in jumbo squid over a more generic one, such as ensemble modelling and model averaging (Punt et al. 2016; Buckland et al. 1997), is that it ties three concrete, actionable components together: identifying alternative hypotheses about population dynamics, weighting those hypotheses based on model reliability and expert judgment, and combining the resulting distributions into probability statements that can directly inform harvest control rules or other management decisions.

This summary condenses the working paper, Reconciling competing stock assessments for jumbo flying squid through probabilistic risk analysis, archived at 10.5281/zenodo.21361536.

title: Slides
text:
A presentation for the SPRFMO Third Squid Workshop (SCW18), drawing together the material on this site: why the three 2025 stock assessments of jumbo flying squid (Dosidicus gigas) disagree, and how a probabilistic risk-analysis framework can reconcile them.
The deck walks through each assessment in turn — CALAMASUR, Chile’s SPiCT and China’s Bayesian state-space model — before turning to the risk-analysis framework, with particular attention to how competing hypotheses are hierarchized and weighted.
Open the slides Download PDF






The slides open in a new tab. Use the arrow keys or space to advance, F for full screen, O for a slide overview, and S for speaker notes. The 31-page PDF is the handout version, with all build-up steps shown.

title: Slides
text:
What it covers

 Section 1 · The disagreement Three assessments, three verdicts The same species, region and catch data to 2024 — yet CALAMASUR, Chile and China reach markedly different conclusions on stock status. 
 Section 2 · Why they differ A model-structure spectrum The divergence comes from model architecture and how each treats the 2024 decline and ENSO — not from the underlying data. 
 Section 3 · The framework Hierarchize, weight, combine Carrying the competing models as weighted hypotheses in a three-level tree, then combining them into probability statements that can seed a harvest control rule.

title: Stock Assessment of Jumbo Flying Squid
text: A technical paper by CALAMASUR (Roa-Ureta and Wiff 2025), presented as an observer paper (SC13-Obs03) to the 13th Meeting of the SPRFMO Scientific Committee (8–13 September 2025, Wellington, New Zealand). Dated August 7, 2025.

title: Stock Assessment of Jumbo Flying Squid
text: A technical paper by CALAMASUR (Roa-Ureta and Wiff 2025), presented as an observer paper (SC13-Obs03) to the 13th Meeting of the SPRFMO Scientific Committee (8–13 September 2025, Wellington, New Zealand). Dated August 7, 2025.

title: Stock Assessment of Jumbo Flying Squid
text:
2 Subject
A region-wide stock assessment of jumbo squid (Dosidicus gigas) across the South East Pacific (SEP) — the world’s largest invertebrate fishery (over 1 million tonnes in recent years, 15th largest of all fisheries). It is fished in Ecuadorian, Peruvian, and Chilean EEZs plus international high seas (currently all Chinese vessels; Chinese Taipei operated until 2020, South Korea until 2019).

title: Stock Assessment of Jumbo Flying Squid
text:
3 Methods
A two-stage, environment-driven approach spanning 1990–2024:

Stage 1 fits multi-annual, multi-fleet generalized depletion models to a monthly database (Jan 2012–Dec 2024) of total catch, fishing effort, and mean weight, for three fleets: Peruvian, Chilean, and Asian. The database covers >99% of regional landings.
Stage 2 uses a Pella-Tomlinson surplus production model driven by El Niño/La Niña cycles (NOAA’s Oceanic Niño Index, 10 transitions 1990–2024), testing 16 hypotheses about how carrying capacity, production symmetry, and intrinsic growth rate change with the environment.

title: Stock Assessment of Jumbo Flying Squid
text:
4 Key results

Depletion models fit Chilean and Asian data well, Peruvian data less so (fits ~90% of points).
Natural mortality estimated at 0.17/month (≈2.0/year), consistent with the species’ short life cycle.
Biomass fluctuates widely (maxima an order of magnitude above minima) but shows no sustained downward trend; highest peaks were before 2019.
Exploitation rates have risen since 2020 but remain mostly below 40% (peaked ~40% in 2018, now ~20–30%).
The best-supported hypothesis is that only the intrinsic growth rate r varies with the environment — higher during El Niño (2.93/yr) than La Niña (0.48/yr).

title: Stock Assessment of Jumbo Flying Squid
text:
5 Main conclusion
The stock is not overfished, though it may be undergoing overfishing. Because the stock has entered a regime of strong environment-driven fluctuations, MSY and BMSY are judged inadequate reference points, and a standard Kobe plot cannot be built. During El Niño years the model even shows negative surplus production. Estimates of sustainable harvest rates (mean latent productivity) remain statistically imprecise.

title: Stock Assessment of Jumbo Flying Squid
text:
6 Way forward
Develop models with 4 fleets and 3 natural-mortality zones (Peru, Chile, high seas), and move the surplus-production model to a continuous, monthly ONI-driven time step. Better environmental reference points are needed to properly judge exploitation status.

title: Stock Assessment of Jumbo Flying Squid
text:
References


Roa-Ureta, Rubén H., and Rodrigo Wiff. 2025. Stock Assessment of the Jumbo Squid (Dosidicus gigas) in the Eastern Pacific with Multi-Annual Multi-Fleet Generalized Depletion Models Combined with Environmental Surplus Production Models (1990–2024). Scientific Committee Document Nos. SC13-Obs03. South Pacific Regional Fisheries Management Organisation (SPRFMO).

title: Assessment of Jumbo Squid in the SE Pacific — State-Space Biomass Dynamics Model & ENSO Impacts
text: A stock assessment paper by China (Li and Chen 2025) (Gang Li & Xinjun Chen, National Data Centre for Distant-water Fisheries of China / Shanghai Ocean University), presented as SC13-SQ07 to the 13th Meeting of the SPRFMO Scientific Committee (8–13 September 2025, Wellington, New Zealand).

title: Assessment of Jumbo Squid in the SE Pacific — State-Space Biomass Dynamics Model & ENSO Impacts
text: A stock assessment paper by China (Li and Chen 2025) (Gang Li & Xinjun Chen, National Data Centre for Distant-water Fisheries of China / Shanghai Ocean University), presented as SC13-SQ07 to the 13th Meeting of the SPRFMO Scientific Committee (8–13 September 2025, Wellington, New Zealand).

title: Assessment of Jumbo Squid in the SE Pacific — State-Space Biomass Dynamics Model & ENSO Impacts
text:
2 Subject
A regional stock assessment of jumbo squid (Dosidicus gigas) in the Southeast Pacific, combining catch and multi-fleet abundance indices with environmental drivers. Its distinguishing feature is an explicit test of how the El Niño–La Niña (ENSO) cycle affects population dynamics, treating D. gigas as a single stock unit across the region (per recent genetic evidence).

title: Assessment of Jumbo Squid in the SE Pacific — State-Space Biomass Dynamics Model & ENSO Impacts
text:
3 Methods
A Bayesian state-space surplus production (Pella-Tomlinson) model, fitted in WinBUGS via MCMC. Four abundance indices were used: Peruvian and Chilean CPUE from CALAMASUR, plus the Chinese squid-jigging fleet CPUE standardized with a Generalized Additive Model (GAM) using a gamma distribution and environmental covariates (SST, chlorophyll-a, salinity, Niño 1+2 index) and spatiotemporal terms. Catch data (2012–2024) came from FAO plus national statistics. Seven environment-dependent models were tested, letting carrying capacity K, intrinsic growth r, and shape s vary between El Niño and La Niña phases (defined by Niño 1+2 SST anomalies). One base scenario plus three prior-sensitivity scenarios were run.

title: Assessment of Jumbo Squid in the SE Pacific — State-Space Biomass Dynamics Model & ENSO Impacts
text:
4 Key results

The GAM confirmed year, month and salinity as the main CPUE drivers; nominal and standardized CPUE both peaked in 2015 and were lowest in 2024.
Among the seven environment-dependent models, the Kr model (both K and r vary with ENSO) had the lowest DIC and was best supported — climate drives fluctuations in both carrying capacity and growth rate.
Base-scenario MSY ≈ 1.19–1.35 million tonnes (traditional model); the environment-dependent model gave MSY around 1.3–1.6 Mt, differing between El Niño and La Niña phases.
B2012/K ≈ 0.5, indicating the stock was at roughly half of unexploited biomass at the start of the series.
Kobe-plot diagnostics show the stock has been sustainable since 2013 — no overfishing and not overfished. Terminal-year probability of healthy status was 58.6–73.1% (traditional model) and 70.9–88.3% (environment-dependent model).

title: Assessment of Jumbo Squid in the SE Pacific — State-Space Biomass Dynamics Model & ENSO Impacts
text:
5 Main conclusion
The stock is neither overfished nor undergoing overfishing, and has remained healthy since 2013 with catch consistently below MSY. Notably, despite the sharp decline in D. gigas landings in 2024, the stock stayed healthy — a pattern the authors link to the influence of El Niño on population dynamics. The environment-dependent (Kr) model reinforces that climate-driven variability in carrying capacity and growth rate is central to the stock’s behaviour.

title: Assessment of Jumbo Squid in the SE Pacific — State-Space Biomass Dynamics Model & ENSO Impacts
text:
6 Way forward
Continue integrating environmental (ENSO) drivers into the assessment, given the strong sensitivity of carrying capacity and growth rate to climate. Further refinement of the standardized CPUE indices and the environment-dependent model structure would improve reference points and terminal-year status estimates.

title: Assessment of Jumbo Squid in the SE Pacific — State-Space Biomass Dynamics Model & ENSO Impacts
text:
References


Li, Gang, and Xinjun Chen. 2025. Assessment of Jumbo Squid in Southeast Pacific Ocean Based on State-Space Biomass Dynamics Model and Impacts of ENSO. Scientific Committee Document Nos. SC13-SQ07. National Data Centre for Distant-water Fisheries of China, Shanghai Ocean University.
